planningModel.intent: Guide current-phase decisions, trigger checks, sequencing, and buy-ahead planning without becoming a rigid weekly checklist.

itemTypes.label: Weather
itemTypes.meaning: Decision depends on forecast, soil moisture, temperature, wind, or rain.

itemTypes.label: Conditional
itemTypes.meaning: Do only if observed conditions justify it.

itemTypes.label: Buy ahead
itemTypes.meaning: Purchase or stage materials before the work window.

phases.label: Late Feb-Mar Prep
phases.summary: Prep the season before spring timing gets tight.
phases.mainRecommendation: Inspect systems, watch warm 50°F+ treatment days, and buy spring materials before the April rush.

items.title: Season prep
items.guidance: Service mower, review last year, and start scouting turf condition.

items.title: Inspect irrigation
items.guidance: Look for obvious repairs before startup decisions matter.
items.trigger: Do not fully activate while hard-freeze risk remains.

items.title: Broadleaf spot treatment
items.guidance: Spot treat only on label-safe 50°F+ days.
items.trigger: Use calm weather and active weeds.

items.title: Stage spring materials
items.guidance: Buy pre-emergent, calibration supplies, repair parts, and early-season products.
items.buyAhead: 4-6 weeks before the April application window.

phases.label: Pre-emergent Window
phases.summary: The spring prevention window is about timing, water-in, and avoiding unnecessary fertilizer push.
phases.mainRecommendation: Decide on crabgrass preventer now; buy product, apply around redbud bloom or mid-April timing, then water it in.

items.title: Crabgrass preventer
items.guidance: April 15 is a common Kansas target; eastern redbud bloom is the practical cue.
items.sequence: Buy product -> apply on a calm day -> water in.

items.title: Water-in timing
items.guidance: Use rain or irrigation to activate product, but avoid storm washout.
items.trigger: Apply ahead of gentle rain or water according to label.

items.title: Hold fertilizer push
items.guidance: Pre-emergent comes before spring fertilizer. Spring nitrogen is optional for fescue.
items.condition: Feed only if turf condition and summer follow-through justify it.

items.title: Pre-emergent supply
items.guidance: Two 5,000 sq ft units are the full-property planning assumption.

phases.label: Late Apr-May Adjust
phases.summary: Follow-up feeding, weed control, irrigation activation, and early insect planning become the decision points.
phases.mainRecommendation: Check irrigation coverage and use fertilizer or weed/feed only if label conditions and lawn needs fit.

items.title: Optional spring feed
items.guidance: Spring fertilizer matters less than fall for tall fescue.
items.condition: Use only with realistic watering and mowing follow-through.

items.title: Weed/feed label timing
items.guidance: Watering and rain timing depend on the exact product label.
items.trigger: Treat only when weeds are present and conditions fit.

items.title: Sprinkler startup
items.guidance: Activate and adjust after freeze risk passes, then check coverage.

items.title: Warm-season pest supplies
items.guidance: Stage mosquito control and perimeter insect barrier products before pressure ramps.
items.buyAhead: Before spring rains and midsummer refresh windows.

phases.label: May-Jun Conditional
phases.summary: Grub prevention is a conditional lane, not an automatic lawn program.
phases.mainRecommendation: Use grub preventer only if prior grub history or observed risk justifies it.

items.title: Grub preventer lane
items.guidance: Preventive grub products are for known history, not routine guessing.
items.condition: Use only with prior grub history or strong risk evidence.

items.title: Dylox only for active damage
items.guidance: Curative grub treatment is only for active grub damage.

phases.label: Bagworm Watch
phases.summary: Scout susceptible trees and shrubs while larvae are small enough to manage.
phases.mainRecommendation: Inspect evergreens and susceptible shrubs, then treat only if activity is visible.

items.title: Bagworm scouting
items.guidance: Look for active small larvae on susceptible plants.
items.condition: Treat only when activity is confirmed.

items.title: Bagworm spray ready
items.guidance: Have treatment available before June if susceptible plants have a history.

phases.label: Summer Setup
phases.summary: Shift from improvement to protection: water deeply, mow high, and avoid pushing fescue in heat.
phases.mainRecommendation: Consider a second pre-emergent only if the first product was short-duration; otherwise move into stress management.

items.title: Second pre-emergent check
items.guidance: Many products weaken after about 60 days.
items.condition: Skip if a season-long product was used.

items.title: Deep watering rhythm
items.guidance: Water deeply and infrequently when turf actually needs it.
items.trigger: Adjust for rain, heat stress, and soil moisture.

items.title: Higher mowing
items.guidance: Keep fescue taller through heat and avoid aggressive improvement work.

items.title: Mosquito pressure
items.guidance: Mosquito work becomes recurring after warm rain patterns.

items.title: Perimeter barrier cadence
items.guidance: Refresh perimeter insect barrier only when pest pressure and label timing justify it.
items.condition: Spring and midsummer refreshes depend on observed pressure.

phases.label: Fall Prep
phases.summary: Decide what recovery work September deserves before products disappear or schedules tighten.
phases.mainRecommendation: Assess summer thinning, stage seed/fertilizer, and plan irrigation support before Labor Day.

items.title: Thin-area assessment
items.guidance: Overseed only if summer thinning created a real target.
items.condition: Mark repair zones before buying seed.

items.title: Fall materials
items.guidance: Buy seed, fertilizer, and patch materials before September demand.

phases.label: September Build
phases.summary: September is the highest-value tall fescue month: feed, repair, and recover.
phases.mainRecommendation: Prioritize Labor Day fertilization and any realistic repair or overseeding work.

items.title: Labor Day fertilizer
items.guidance: This is one of the major annual fescue checkpoints.
items.sequence: Feed when heat is easing and irrigation support is realistic.

items.title: Repair / overseed
items.guidance: Repair thin areas only where watering follow-through is realistic.
items.condition: Use if summer thinning or bare areas justify it.

items.title: Seed watering
items.guidance: New seed needs consistent moisture; weather should drive irrigation adjustments.
items.trigger: Warm dry spells require more frequent light watering.

phases.label: November Closeout
phases.summary: The second major fescue checkpoint: late fall nitrogen, leaves, and broadleaf control opportunity.
phases.mainRecommendation: Do the November feed if conditions fit, manage leaves, and use fall broadleaf control only when weeds and label conditions align.

items.title: November fertilizer
items.guidance: This matters more than chasing summer color.
items.sequence: Apply before winter closeout when turf can use it.

items.title: Fall broadleaf control
items.guidance: Fall is often a good broadleaf control opportunity.
items.condition: Treat only if weeds are present and label conditions fit.

items.title: Winterizer supply
items.guidance: Two 5,000 sq ft units are the full-property planning assumption.
items.buyAhead: Before November leaf cleanup compresses the schedule.
